facts_disclosed: Moved to coaching U-17s — 3 hamstring injuries last season

turns.content: man i need some help planning a warm up routine. like a proper structured one not just jog round the pitch twice and do some stretches you know
Sure, I can help with that. What's the context -- is this for a team, a gym session, or something else? And what kind of activity are they warming up for?
its for the lads i coach. football. thing is right ive just moved up to the U-17s and i need to come correct because the last coach was a bit relaxed with warmups tbh and it showed

Got it, so you're starting fresh with a new age group and want to set the standard early. Makes sense. What kind of issues came from the relaxed warm-ups? Any specific injuries or just general poor preparation?

bruv 3 of them did their hamstrings last season. THREE. in one season thats mad. and these are 16 17 year old lads who should be bouncing not pulling up with muscle injuries every other week. like thats a prevention failure init not just bad luck

Three hamstring injuries in one season for a youth team is a big red flag -- you're right, that points to a systemic issue, not just bad luck. At that age they're growing fast too, which adds extra demand on the posterior chain.

So you're looking at a warm-up that specifically targets hamstring injury prevention. Are you familiar with the FIFA 11+ programme? It was designed exactly for this.

yeah i know 11+ from uni actually. we covered it in my sports rehab module. the evidence is proper strong for reducing hamstring and ACL injuries. i want to use that as a base but adapt it a bit because some of the exercises are bare basic for U-17s who should be able to handle more

Smart approach -- using 11+ as a framework but progressing the difficulty. For U-17s you can definitely push the intensity. A few ideas for building on it:

- **Nordic hamstring curls** -- the single best exercise for hamstring injury prevention. The evidence is overwhelming. Start with 3-4 reps and build up.
- **Single-leg Romanian deadlifts** -- challenges eccentric hamstring strength plus balance.
- **Progressive running drills** -- not just jogging, but building through 50%, 75%, 90% effort with direction changes.
- **Hip flexor and glute activation** -- banded walks, clamshells. Weak glutes overload the hamstrings.

You could structure it as a 15-minute block: 5 minutes movement prep, 5 minutes strength activation, 5 minutes sport-specific running. Would that kind of structure work for your sessions?

thats sick yeah exactly what im thinking. nordics are class but these lads have never done them before so theyre going to hate me for the first few weeks haha. the thing is i study this stuff you know like literally this is what my degree is in so it made sense for me to take over from the other coach. the U-14s were proper gutted when i told them i was moving up but the older lads need me more init

Ha, yeah nordics are brutal when you first start -- expect a lot of face-planting into the grass. But they'll thank you when they're not missing 6 weeks with a pulled hamstring.

And it sounds like a natural fit. Having someone coaching who actually understands the anatomy and the evidence behind injury prevention is massive for that age group. They're at the stage where bad habits become real injuries, especially if they're playing at a competitive level.

For introducing nordics, start them with a small range of motion -- just the top third of the movement. Get them used to the eccentric load before you let them go full range. Partner-assisted so someone holds their ankles. You'll probably need to demo it yourself a few times.

yeah i was thinking partner assisted for the first couple weeks then maybe add a band for support. tbh i also want to do some education with them not just exercises. like explain WHY we're doing this because if they understand that their hamstrings are under more stress during sprinting and deceleration theyll actually buy into the warmup instead of treating it like a chore

That's a really mature coaching approach. Getting buy-in through understanding rather than just compliance makes a huge difference, especially with teenagers. If they know the why, they'll push themselves harder during the warm-up instead of going through the motions.

You could keep it simple and visual:
- Show them the sprint biomechanics -- hamstrings work hardest during the late swing phase when the leg is decelerating before foot strike
- Use the stat: teams that do nordic curls consistently see up to 50% fewer hamstring injuries
- Make it personal -- ask who wants to miss 6 weeks of the season

Do you want me to help you put together a session plan for the first few weeks? Like a phased progression?

yes bruv that would be bare helpful. like a 4 week plan where week 1 is intro level and by week 4 theyre doing the full routine. i want it written out so i can print it and stick it on the clipboard you know

Here's a 4-week progressive warm-up plan:

**Week 1 -- Introduction (12 mins)**
- 3 min light jog with dynamic stretches (leg swings, hip circles)
- 3 min glute activation (banded walks x10 each way, clamshells x10 each side)
- 3 min partner nordic curls (top third ROM only, 3 reps x 2 sets)
- 3 min progressive runs (3 x 40m at 50%, 70%, 85%)

**Week 2 -- Build (14 mins)**
- 3 min jog with dynamic stretches + high knees, heel flicks
- 3 min glute/hip activation (add single-leg glute bridges x8 each)
- 4 min nordics (half ROM, 4 reps x 2 sets) + single-leg RDL (bodyweight, 6 each)
- 4 min progressive runs with direction changes (cut left, cut right)

**Week 3 -- Progress (15 mins)**
- 3 min movement prep (add lateral shuffles, carioca)
- 3 min activation (banded walks, single-leg bridges with 3-sec hold)
- 5 min nordics (full ROM attempt, 4 reps x 3 sets) + single-leg RDL with football hold
- 4 min running (acceleration/deceleration drills, 3 x sprint to stop)

**Week 4 -- Full Protocol (15 mins)**
- 3 min dynamic movement prep (all movements combined)
- 4 min strength activation (nordics 5 x 3, RDLs, Copenhagen plank 20 sec each side)
- 4 min agility (ladder drills, reactive sprints)
- 4 min match-intensity running with ball

Print-friendly enough for the clipboard?

thats quality mate. copenhagen plank is a shout as well for the adductors. groin injuries were a problem too but not as bad as the hammies. im going to adapt this slightly but the structure is spot on. first session with the U-17s is tuesday so ill trial week 1 then

Sounds like you've got a solid plan. Adapt it to what you see on the ground -- if some of the lads are more advanced you can push them to week 2 exercises early and keep others on the intro level. Differentiation matters even in a warm-up.

Good shout on the Copenhagen plank too -- adductor injuries are the second most common in youth football after hamstrings, so covering both is smart.

Good luck on Tuesday. Let the lads know early that this is how every session starts from now on. Set the standard from day one.
100% thats the plan. set the tone early. cheers for this man genuinely helpful. right i need to go write this up properly before i forget the details. safe bruv
No worries, glad it helped. Get it written up and laminated if you can -- saves it getting rained on knowing Bristol weather. Good luck with the U-17s, sounds like they're in good hands. Take care.
